figures.caption: Figure 2. Family-wise comparison of H prompt stability and N prompt stability, for severity α = 5. Values close to 1 indicate that the leading prompt is largely invariant to the corruption operator.
Figure 3. Severity trends differ across corruption families. Similar monotonicity does not imply similar safety impact.

Table 1. Family-wise coupling between embedding drift and decision drift, with 95% confidence intervals on Spearman (ρ) and Pearson (r) correlation coefficients. FPR and FNR denote negative-to-positive and positive-to-negative hazard verdict flips, respectively. H and N stability measure the proportion of images whose leading prompt is unchanged under corruption.
Figure 4. Embedding drift vs. margin drift across corruption families. Points are colored by corruption family in (a) and by severity level in (b)–(d).
Figure 5. Top 4 most safe (top row) and hazardous (bottom row) images in the dataset, based on hazard score Λ.
Figure 6. Visualizations of image corruptions on randomly-selected road images. Hazard level Λ, embedding drift ∆, and margin drift DΛ are overlaid on each image.
